name: nergal (war/plague god)
shortDescription: Nergal was a Mesopotamian god associated with war, plague, pestilence, and the underworld. He was a powerful and fearsome deity, often depicted as a consort or subordinate of other major gods.
description: Nergal was a significant deity in the Mesopotamian pantheon, particularly in Sumerian and Akkadian mythology. He was primarily associated with war, pestilence, plague, and the underworld. His character was complex, embodying both destructive forces and, at times, a sense of order and justice within the realm of the dead. Nergal's origins are somewhat obscure, but he rose to prominence during the Akkadian period and continued to be worshipped throughout Mesopotamian history. He was often considered the consort of Ereshkigal, the queen of the underworld, although some traditions depict him as forcibly taking her throne or marrying her after a contest. As a god of plague and pestilence, Nergal was feared for his ability to bring disease and suffering upon humanity. However, he was also seen as a protector against these forces, and rituals were performed to appease him and ward off his wrath. His association with war made him a patron deity of warriors and hunters. Depictions of Nergal often portray him as a fierce warrior, sometimes with a lion's head or accompanied by lions. He was also connected to the sun, particularly the scorching midday sun, further emphasizing his association with heat, destruction, and power. While not always a central figure in major myths, Nergal's presence was felt throughout Mesopotamian religious life, representing the darker aspects of the divine and the ever-present threat of death and disease. He was a powerful reminder of the precariousness of human existence and the need to appease the forces that could bring about its end. Later syncretism saw him associated with other deities, including possibly Erra, further blurring the lines between war, plague, and cosmic destruction.

sources.title: Gods, Demons, and Symbols of Ancient Mesopotamia
sources.author: Jeremy Black and Anthony Green
